title: Mixture of Experts: How Models Get Bigger Without Getting Slower
shortDescription: The architecture that allows trillion-parameter models to run at the cost of much smaller ones by activating only a fraction of parameters per token.
content:
The scaling laws of AI suggest that bigger models perform better. But bigger models are more expensive to run — a 1.8 trillion parameter model would require enormous compute for every single token if all parameters were active. Mixture of Experts (MoE) elegantly solves this by having a large total parameter count but only activating a small subset of parameters for each input. Think of it as a company with 100 specialists but only consulting 2-3 of them for any given question.

In a standard Transformer, the feed-forward network (FFN) in each layer processes every token through the same parameters. In an MoE Transformer, the single FFN is replaced with multiple parallel FFNs called 'experts,' plus a small neural network called the 'router' or 'gating network.' For each token, the router examines the token's representation and selects the top-k experts (typically k=2) most relevant to that token. Only these selected experts process the token; the rest remain dormant. The expert outputs are weighted by the router's confidence scores and summed.

Google's Switch Transformer (2021) demonstrated MoE at scale by routing each token to just one expert (top-1 routing), achieving 7x faster pre-training than a comparable dense model. Mistral's Mixtral 8x7B uses 8 experts with top-2 routing, giving it 47 billion total parameters but only 13 billion active parameters per token — performing comparably to LLaMA 2 70B while being dramatically cheaper to run. Google's Gemini 1.5 is reported to use MoE, and GPT-4 is widely believed to be a MoE model with approximately 1.8 trillion total parameters but around 200 billion active per forward pass.

The router's design is the most critical and challenging aspect. A naive router might send most tokens to the same few experts, leaving others underutilized — a problem called 'expert collapse.' To prevent this, systems use load balancing losses that penalize uneven expert utilization. The auxiliary loss adds a small penalty to the training objective when expert load is imbalanced, encouraging the router to distribute tokens more evenly.

Expert specialization emerges naturally during training. Research has shown that different experts tend to specialize in different token types, topics, or linguistic patterns. In multilingual models, some experts specialize in specific languages. In code-generation models, different experts might handle different programming languages or different code patterns (loops, function definitions, error handling). This specialization is not explicitly programmed — it emerges from the training dynamics.

MoE models present unique deployment challenges. While active parameters per token are small, the total model still needs to fit in memory because any expert could be activated at any time. This means a Mixtral 8x7B model needs roughly the same memory as a dense 47B model, even though its computational cost per token is closer to a 13B model. Expert parallelism — distributing different experts across different GPUs — is the standard solution, but it introduces all-to-all communication overhead as tokens need to be routed to the GPU holding their selected expert.

For inference at scale, MoE models require careful expert placement and batching strategies. Since different tokens in a batch might activate different experts, achieving high GPU utilization requires large batch sizes to ensure every expert has enough tokens to process efficiently. Some systems pre-sort tokens by their likely expert routing to improve batching efficiency.

The MoE principle extends beyond its specific architectural implementation. It represents a broader insight about efficient computation: not every input requires the same processing. The best systems route inputs through specialized pathways, activating only the relevant computation. This principle appears in retrieval-augmented generation (routing queries to relevant documents), multi-agent systems (routing tasks to specialized agents), and even in human organizations (routing problems to domain experts).
takeaway: Mixture of Experts decouples model capacity from computational cost by activating only a fraction of parameters per token, enabling models to be simultaneously large (for knowledge capacity) and efficient (for inference speed).

title: Write-Ahead Logging (WAL): How Databases Survive Crashes
shortDescription: The fundamental mechanism that ensures database durability — understanding why data isn't lost when the power goes out.
content:
Every modern database faces a fundamental problem: writes are performed in memory (because it's fast) but must eventually reach disk (because it's durable). If the system crashes between these two events, data is lost. Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) solves this by enforcing a simple rule: before any change is applied to the actual data pages, a record of that change must first be written to a sequential log on disk.

The WAL protocol works as follows. When a transaction modifies data, the database first writes a log record describing the change (e.g., 'page 42, offset 128, old value: 100, new value: 200'). This log record is written sequentially to the WAL file. Only after the log record is safely on disk (confirmed by an fsync) does the database apply the actual change to the in-memory data page. The data page itself may not be written to disk until much later — minutes, hours, or whenever the buffer pool needs to evict it.

If the system crashes, recovery is straightforward. The database reads the WAL from the beginning (or from the last checkpoint) and replays all committed transactions whose changes may not have reached the data files. For uncommitted transactions found in the log, it performs an undo operation using the old values recorded in the log records. This is called ARIES (Algorithm for Recovery and Isolation Exploiting Semantics), developed at IBM Research and used in virtually every major RDBMS.

Why is sequential logging faster than random writes to data pages? Data pages are scattered across the disk — updating page 42, then page 1,847, then page 3 requires the disk head to seek back and forth (on HDDs) or requires multiple random I/O operations (on SSDs). WAL writes, by contrast, are purely sequential — each new record is appended to the end of the log file. Sequential writes are 100-1000x faster than random writes on HDDs and 3-10x faster on SSDs. By converting random writes into sequential writes, WAL transforms a slow operation into a fast one.

Checkpointing is the complementary process that prevents the WAL from growing forever. Periodically, the database flushes all dirty (modified) in-memory pages to their actual locations in the data files, then records a checkpoint in the WAL. After a checkpoint, all log records before it can be safely deleted because the changes they describe are now in the data files. The checkpoint frequency represents a tradeoff: more frequent checkpoints reduce recovery time (less WAL to replay) but increase I/O overhead during normal operation.

PostgreSQL's WAL implementation is a textbook example. PostgreSQL writes WAL records in 8KB segments, grouped into 16MB segment files. The pg_wal directory on a busy PostgreSQL server might contain dozens of these segment files. WAL records include enough information to redo any operation: INSERT records contain the full tuple, UPDATE records contain both old and new values, DELETE records contain the deleted tuple. PostgreSQL's checkpoint process (the checkpointer) periodically flushes dirty buffers and advances the checkpoint location.

WAL enables another crucial feature: replication. Since the WAL contains a complete, ordered record of every change to the database, streaming it to a replica server allows that server to maintain an exact copy. PostgreSQL's streaming replication, MySQL's binary log replication, and SQL Server's log shipping all work on this principle. The WAL is essentially a change data capture (CDC) stream built into the database engine.

Beyond traditional databases, the WAL principle appears throughout distributed systems. Apache Kafka is essentially a distributed WAL — an append-only, sequentially written log of events. Redis uses an Append-Only File (AOF) that is conceptually identical to a WAL. Event sourcing architectures treat the event log as the source of truth and derive current state by replaying events — the same pattern as WAL recovery. Understanding WAL is understanding the fundamental mechanism by which any stateful system achieves durability: write your intentions sequentially before acting on them, and you can always recover your state.
takeaway: Write-Ahead Logging converts the expensive problem of random durable writes into cheap sequential appends, while providing the foundation for crash recovery, replication, and change data capture — it's the most important primitive in durable data systems.

title: Flywheel Models: Building Businesses That Accelerate Themselves
shortDescription: How Amazon, Uber, and other dominant companies create virtuous cycles where each component of the business reinforces the others.
content:
A flywheel is a self-reinforcing loop where the output of each step becomes the input that accelerates the next step. Unlike linear strategies that require proportionally more effort for each unit of growth, flywheels compound — each revolution makes the next revolution easier. Jim Collins popularized the concept in 'Good to Great,' but Amazon operationalized it more effectively than perhaps any company in history.

Amazon's flywheel, sketched by Jeff Bezos on a napkin, works like this: lower prices attract more customers. More customers attract more third-party sellers. More sellers increase selection. More selection improves customer experience. Better experience drives more traffic. More traffic lowers per-unit costs through economies of scale. Lower costs enable even lower prices. Each element feeds the next in an accelerating loop. The brilliance is that Amazon didn't need to invest proportionally more to grow — the flywheel's own momentum carried growth forward.

Uber's flywheel operates similarly: more drivers reduce wait times. Shorter wait times attract more riders. More riders increase driver utilization and earnings. Better earnings attract more drivers. Each additional driver and rider makes the platform slightly better for everyone else, creating a compounding advantage.

The key insight about flywheels is that the hardest part is the beginning. Getting a massive flywheel moving from a standstill requires enormous initial effort with little visible result. The first few rotations feel unrewarding — you're pushing hard and nothing seems to be happening. But each push adds energy, and eventually the flywheel's own momentum begins to help. This is why so many startups fail in the early stages — they give up before the flywheel gains enough momentum to sustain itself.

Building a flywheel requires identifying the reinforcing loops in your business and deliberately designing each component to feed the others. Start by mapping your value chain: what makes customers come? What makes them stay? What makes them bring others? What makes your unit economics improve with scale? Then look for the connections — where does output from one component become input for another?

Not every positive feedback loop is a flywheel. A true flywheel has two properties: each component structurally connects to the next (not just coincidentally), and the cycle accelerates with each revolution (there are increasing returns to scale somewhere in the loop). If more users don't actually make the product better for other users, you don't have a flywheel — you just have growth.

Flywheels can also work in reverse. If Netflix raises prices too much, some subscribers cancel. Fewer subscribers mean less revenue for content. Less content makes the service less attractive. Fewer subscribers cancel. This is a 'doom loop' — the same structure working in the opposite direction. The strategic implication is that you should never weaken any component of your flywheel to optimize for short-term results, because the compounding effects will punish you in the medium term.

For technical leaders, the flywheel concept applies directly to engineering organizations. Better engineering practices attract better engineers. Better engineers write better code. Better code means fewer production issues. Fewer issues mean more time for new features. More features attract more customers. More customers mean more revenue. More revenue funds better engineering hiring. Understanding this flywheel explains why engineering culture is not a 'nice to have' but a strategic asset — it's the starting push that sets the entire organizational flywheel in motion.
takeaway: A flywheel isn't just a growth strategy — it's a structural design where every component of the business feeds another, creating compounding momentum that makes each unit of effort progressively more productive.

title: Executive Communication: Leading with the Bottom Line
shortDescription: How to communicate with senior leaders using the BLUF framework — because executives don't have time for your preamble.
content:
The single biggest communication mistake engineers make when talking to executives is burying the lead. Engineers are trained to build arguments from first principles — lay the foundation, present the evidence, develop the logic, then reach the conclusion. Executives want the opposite: tell me the answer first, then I'll decide if I need the supporting details. This pattern is called BLUF — Bottom Line Up Front — and mastering it is the fastest way to build credibility with senior leaders.

The BLUF structure is: State your recommendation or key finding in the first sentence. Then provide 2-3 supporting points. Then offer to go deeper on any point. An executive's time is their scarcest resource — they might have 30 seconds of attention for your topic before their mind moves to the next of 50 things on their plate. If your main point is in paragraph three, they'll never hear it.

Here are conversation scripts demonstrating BLUF in action:

--- SCENARIO 1: Reporting a production incident to your VP ---

BAD approach: 'So last night around 2 AM, our monitoring detected an anomaly in the payment service. We investigated and found that the connection pool was exhausted because of a configuration change that was deployed yesterday. The deployment had passed all tests but the load test didn't account for the new traffic pattern from the holiday sale. We're seeing about a 5% failure rate on checkouts...'

GOOD approach (BLUF): 'Our checkout failure rate is currently 5%, directly impacting revenue. Root cause is identified — an exhausted connection pool from yesterday's deployment. We have a fix ready that will be deployed within the hour. No data was lost. I'll send a full incident report by end of day.'

Notice the BLUF version: impact first, root cause second, remediation third, details deferred. The VP gets everything they need in four sentences.

--- SCENARIO 2: Proposing a technical investment ---

BAD approach: 'I've been looking at our infrastructure costs. We're using a lot of EC2 instances. Some of them are oversized. The reserved instances are expiring. We could save money with Graviton instances. Also Spot instances could help. And maybe we should look at Kubernetes...'

GOOD approach (BLUF): 'I can reduce our infrastructure costs by 35% — roughly ₹40 lakh annually — through three changes: right-sizing our EC2 fleet, migrating to ARM-based Graviton instances, and adopting Spot instances for batch workloads. I need two engineers for six weeks to execute this. The payback period is under three months.'

The executive heard: savings amount, investment required, payback period. That's a decision-ready pitch in three sentences.

--- SCENARIO 3: Weekly status update email ---

BAD: A multi-paragraph narrative of what your team did this week.

GOOD structure:
'Status: ON TRACK (or AT RISK or BLOCKED)
Key update: The user migration is 70% complete, ahead of schedule.
Risk: Third-party API rate limits may slow the final 30%. Mitigation plan in place.
Decision needed: None this week.
Detailed progress: [link to doc for those who want details]'

The principle behind BLUF extends beyond structure. It requires clarity of thought. You cannot lead with your bottom line if you don't know what your bottom line is. The discipline of forcing yourself to write the first sentence — 'What is the single most important thing I need this person to know?' — forces you to think through your message before delivering it.

Two additional rules for executive communication: First, quantify everything. Don't say 'it's slow' — say 'P99 latency increased from 200ms to 1.2 seconds.' Don't say 'it'll save money' — say 'it'll save ₹40 lakh annually.' Numbers create credibility. Second, always state what you need from them. Are you informing them? Asking for a decision? Requesting resources? Making this explicit respects their time and prevents the meeting from drifting.

As you grow into senior roles, the ability to communicate upward effectively becomes as important as your technical skills. An engineer who can solve hard problems AND communicate the impact clearly is worth twice as much as one who can only do the former.
takeaway: Lead with the answer, not the analysis. Executives need your conclusion in the first sentence, your supporting evidence in the next two, and the option to go deeper — not a journey through your thought process.

title: Asset Allocation: The Decision That Determines 90% of Your Returns
shortDescription: Why how you divide your money between asset classes matters far more than which specific stocks or funds you pick.
content:
A landmark study by Brinson, Hood, and Beebower found that asset allocation — the decision of how to divide your portfolio among stocks, bonds, real estate, gold, and other asset classes — explains approximately 90% of the variability in a portfolio's returns over time. Not stock selection. Not market timing. Asset allocation.

This is one of the most counterintuitive findings in finance. People spend hours researching individual stocks while giving almost no thought to their overall allocation. But whether you put 80% in equities or 40% will affect your long-term returns far more than whether you pick Infosys or TCS within that equity allocation.

The core principle is that different asset classes have different risk-return profiles and, crucially, they don't move in perfect sync with each other (they have low correlation). When Indian stocks fall during a domestic recession, international stocks or gold may hold steady or even rise. By combining assets with different behavior patterns, you can achieve better risk-adjusted returns than any single asset class alone. This is the essence of diversification — not just 'don't put all your eggs in one basket,' but specifically 'put your eggs in baskets that react differently to the same events.'

For a 24-year-old with a high income and a long investment horizon, the allocation should be heavily weighted toward equities because you have decades for the market to recover from any downturn. A reasonable starting allocation might be: 50% Indian equity index (Nifty 50 + Nifty Next 50), 20% international equity (S&P 500 or total world index), 10% gold (Sovereign Gold Bonds or gold ETF), and 20% debt (short-duration debt fund or PPF). As you age and your time horizon shortens, gradually shift from equities toward debt — a process called 'glide path.'

The equity component provides growth. Over the past 20 years, the Nifty 50 has delivered roughly 12-14% annualized returns. The debt component provides stability and acts as 'dry powder' — money you can deploy into equities during major market crashes (which are actually buying opportunities if your horizon is long). Gold provides inflation protection and tends to perform well during periods of economic uncertainty when equities struggle. International equity provides geographic diversification and exposure to the world's best companies.

Rebalancing is the discipline that makes asset allocation work. Over time, your allocation will drift as different assets perform differently. If Indian equities have a great year, they might grow from 50% to 60% of your portfolio. Rebalancing means selling some equity and buying more of the underperforming assets to return to your target allocation. This feels counterintuitive — you're selling winners and buying losers — but it systematically enforces 'buy low, sell high.' Research shows that disciplined annual rebalancing can add 0.5-1% per year to portfolio returns while reducing risk.

The biggest risk in asset allocation is not market volatility — it's your own behavior. Studies consistently show that the average investor earns significantly less than the funds they invest in because they buy after prices have risen (FOMO) and sell after prices have fallen (panic). The purpose of having a written asset allocation plan is to provide a decision framework that overrides emotional impulses. When the market drops 30%, your plan tells you to rebalance by buying equities — exactly when your instincts scream to sell everything.

One often-overlooked asset for young professionals is your own career capital. Your future earning potential is essentially a large, illiquid asset. If you work in technology, your income is already correlated with the tech sector. This means your financial portfolio should arguably be less concentrated in tech stocks than average, because a tech downturn would simultaneously reduce both your portfolio value and your income. This principle — considering your human capital alongside your financial capital — is a sophisticated but important nuance of personal asset allocation.
takeaway: The split between equities, debt, gold, and other asset classes determines 90% of your portfolio's long-term performance — spend your time getting this allocation right and rebalancing regularly, not picking individual securities.

title: Cognitive Biases: The Systematic Errors in Human Thinking
shortDescription: A map of the most dangerous cognitive biases that silently corrupt your decisions in engineering, business, and life.
content:
Your brain is not a rational computer. It's a pattern-matching machine that evolved to make fast decisions in dangerous environments, optimized for survival rather than accuracy. Cognitive biases are the systematic shortcuts (heuristics) that usually serve us well but reliably fail in specific, predictable ways. Understanding these biases doesn't make you immune to them — but it gives you the ability to design systems and processes that compensate for them.

Confirmation bias is arguably the most dangerous bias for technical professionals. It's the tendency to seek, interpret, and remember information that confirms your existing beliefs while ignoring contradictory evidence. When you believe a bug is in the database layer, you'll subconsciously focus on database-related clues and dismiss evidence pointing elsewhere. When you believe a technical approach will work, you'll notice papers supporting it and skip those that highlight its limitations. The antidote is actively seeking disconfirming evidence: before committing to a hypothesis, ask 'What evidence would convince me I'm wrong?' and then look for exactly that evidence.

Anchoring bias causes your estimates and decisions to be disproportionately influenced by the first number or piece of information you encounter. If a colleague says 'I think this will take six months,' subsequent estimates from the entire team will cluster around six months, regardless of independent analysis. In salary negotiations, the first number mentioned (the 'anchor') heavily influences the final outcome. To combat anchoring, generate your own estimate before hearing anyone else's, and when negotiating, try to set the anchor yourself.

Survivorship bias is the tendency to focus on entities that passed some selection process while overlooking those that didn't. When studying successful startups, we analyze what they did right — but we don't analyze the thousands of failed startups that did the same things. When you read about a developer who dropped out of college and became a billionaire, you're not hearing about the millions who dropped out and struggled. This bias makes strategies look more effective than they actually are because we only see the survivors.

The sunk cost fallacy drives people to continue investing in a project, relationship, or strategy because of resources already spent, rather than evaluating future prospects objectively. 'We've already spent six months building this service, we can't abandon it now' is sunk cost reasoning. The six months are gone regardless of your decision — the only rational question is: 'Given what we know now, is continuing the best use of our future time?' The most successful companies and leaders are those who can cut losses on sunk costs without emotional attachment.

Availability bias causes people to overestimate the probability of events that are easily recalled — typically because they're recent, dramatic, or emotionally charged. After a major production outage, teams often over-invest in preventing that specific type of failure while ignoring more probable risks. After reading about a company's data breach, you might overestimate the probability of your own company being breached while underestimating the risk of more mundane but more likely failures. Combat this by relying on base rates and data rather than memorable examples.

The planning fallacy — the systematic tendency to underestimate the time, cost, and risk of future actions while overestimating their benefits — is perhaps the most practically relevant bias for engineers. Studies show that software projects are estimated at 50-100% of their actual duration. The reference class forecasting technique helps: instead of estimating from the inside ('this feature needs a database change, an API change, and a UI change — should take a week'), look at the outside view ('how long did similar features actually take historically?'). History is a much better predictor than bottom-up analysis because it automatically accounts for the unexpected complications that bottom-up estimates consistently miss.

The meta-lesson is this: you cannot think your way out of cognitive biases through willpower alone. Instead, build systems that compensate for them. Use checklists to combat confirmation bias during debugging. Use planning poker (where estimates are revealed simultaneously) to prevent anchoring. Use pre-mortems ('imagine this project failed — why?') to counteract the planning fallacy. The organizations that perform best are not those with the smartest individuals, but those with the best bias-correcting systems.
takeaway: Cognitive biases are not character flaws — they're features of human cognition that served our ancestors well but systematically mislead us in modern contexts. The solution is not willpower but designing processes and systems that compensate for predictable irrationality.
